pattern: A node can't be recorded until every one of its subtrees already has been, so the cleanest iterative trick is to collect answers in a convenient order and flip once at the end.
hints: Postorder means every descendant's value appears before its ancestor's — recursion captures this directly by visiting all children first, then the node itself.
An iterative version can push a node, record it immediately, then push its children left-to-right — that builds something close to postorder but reversed.
Reverse the whole collected sequence once the stack empties, and pushing children in original (not reversed) order turns that reversal into exact postorder.

edge_inputs.description: an empty tree has no values to visit
a lone root visits just itself
one root fanning out to nine children, so all nine leaves must appear before the root
a single-child chain six nodes deep, forcing the deepest leaf to be recorded first
an irregular tree with uneven fan-out, testing that every child subtree finishes before its parent is recorded
repeated values across separate nodes so a value-based visited-set would wrongly merge distinct subtrees

stress.description: random n-ary trees grown by attaching each new node to a random existing node with up to 4 children
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randint(1, max(1, min(size, 80)))
    children = {1: []}
    order = [1]
    for v in range(2, n + 1):
        candidates = [p for p in order if len(children[p]) < 4]
        parent = rng.choice(candidates) if candidates else rng.choice(order)
        children[parent].append(v)
        children[v] = []
        order.append(v)
    wire = [1, None]
    queue = [1]
    while queue:
        node = queue.pop(0)
        for k in children[node]:
            wire.append(k)
            queue.append(k)
        wire.append(None)
    while wire and wire[-1] is None:
        wire.pop()
    return [wire]
